hook: A labyrinth of restored colonial tunnels and a buried stream beneath an 1830 San Telmo mansion, excavated over 20 years.
facts: Located at Defensa 755 in San Telmo, Buenos Aires (postcode C1065).
The zanjón (a small stream that once crossed San Telmo) and its brick tunnels were accidentally discovered in 1985 beneath a mansion built around 1830.
After roughly 20 years of private archaeological work, the underground network of tunnels was opened to the public as a museum.
The tunnels expose strata revealing more than four centuries of the city's history, from early waterworks to later cisterns and foundations.
It operates as a museum visitable by guided tour, open daily (commonly listed 11am-5pm).
w: One man set out to restore a derelict San Telmo mansion and found a city beneath it: brick tunnels that once channelled the Zanjon stream, layered with foundations back to the 1500s. Decades of private excavation turned it into a hushed museum of arches and waterways under the pavement. Buenos Aires, drained of its myths and dug back up.

hook: A site museum built around one of the largest colonial cisterns in Latin America, unearthed beneath the block where Rosas once governed.
facts: Located at Moreno 550 in Monserrat, two blocks from Plaza de Mayo.
The on-site museum displays a large viceregal-era cistern (aljibe) described as one of the largest found in Latin America, plus roughly 2,300 archaeological pieces recovered during excavation.

The cistern belonged to the Ezcurra-Rosas family; Juan Manuel de Rosas and Encarnación Ezcurra lived on the site, which served as the Governor's Residence and political centre of the Argentine Confederation in the 1830s-40s.
Recovered artefacts include 19th-century dishware, perfume bottles and animal bone fragments salvaged during construction of the modern Paseo de la Cisterna building.
The museum occupies about 215 square metres within the building complex.
w: When workers cleaned out an old cistern on the site of strongman Juan Manuel de Rosas's family home, they hauled up more than 15,000 broken things — crockery, bottles, toys. The well had quietly swallowed it all. Now displayed where it was found, it reads like an archaeology of porteno daily life. Rubbish, turned treasure.

n: Túneles de la Manzana de las Luces
hook: Jesuit-era underground galleries beneath Buenos Aires' oldest cultural block, reopened to the public for short guided descents.
facts: The Manzana de las Luces is the historic block bounded by Bolívar, Moreno, Alsina and Perú streets in Monserrat, containing the Colegio Nacional de Buenos Aires and the Church of San Ignacio.
A 1780 map ordered by Viceroy Juan José de Vértiz documents the tunnels, attributed to the Jesuits in the 18th century before their expulsion.
In 1981 the Historical Monument designation was extended to the former Jesuit Procuracies at Perú 272 and Alsina.
Public tunnel visits run on weekends in small groups (up to six people, ages 16+) for short durations of about 15 minutes, with prior online reservation required.
The visitor address for the tunnel tours is Perú 222 in Monserrat.
legend: Folklore variously explains the tunnels as escape routes, smuggling passages, weapons caches from the Rosas era, underground prisons, or Masonic galleries; their true purpose remains undocumented.
w: Beneath the oldest block in the city run the brick tunnels of the Manzana de las Luces, dug by the Jesuits in the 1700s and long rumoured to link the churches, colleges and seat of power above. Smugglers and conspirators got blamed for them. Their real purpose stays murky. Descend on a weekend tour into the city's secret wiring.

n: Seminario Metropolitano de la Inmaculada Concepción
hook: The four-block Villa Devoto seminary where a young Jorge Bergoglio trained, hiding tunnels and galleries beneath its halls.
facts: The Seminario Metropolitano de la Inmaculada Concepción occupies four city blocks in Villa Devoto, on José Cubas street.
Jorge Mario Bergoglio, later Pope Francis, studied his early formation here for about two years before continuing with the Jesuits in Chile.
It was the first building purpose-built as a metropolitan seminary in Buenos Aires, replacing earlier adapted spaces dating to the Viceroyalty.
The complex contains tunnels and galleries that are shown on guided tours, which open during the annual 'Semana del Seminario'.
It functions as an active Catholic seminary of the Archdiocese of Buenos Aires.
w: The leafy Devoto seminary where a young Jorge Bergoglio trained for the priesthood — before the world knew him as Pope Francis — hides odd treasures in its basements: cinema projectors, rare incunabula, a restored carriage. Once a year its tunnels and vaults open to visitors. Curiosities below a future pope's old classrooms.

n: Pasaje de la Defensa (Casa de los Ezeiza)
hook: An 1880s Italian-academicist mansion that became a 32-family tenement and is now a restored antiques arcade around three courtyards.
facts: Located at Defensa 1179 in San Telmo, half a block from Plaza Dorrego.
Built in the 1870s-1880s in Italian academicist style for the wealthy Ezeiza family, which is said to have abandoned it during the 1871 yellow fever epidemic.
It later served as a primary school and headquarters of the National Institute for the Deaf-Mute, then during the 1930s became a conventillo (tenement) housing 32 families.
In 1980 it was restored by architects Raúl Servente, Félix Alemán and Juan Firpo, reopening in 1981 as the Galería/Pasaje de la Defensa.
Today it houses an antiques and crafts shopping arcade around three courtyards (del Tiempo, del Árbol and Ezeiza), open Tuesday to Sunday.
w: Built as a grand family house in the 1880s, then carved into a conventillo where dozens of immigrant families shared one courtyard and one tap, this San Telmo arcade now shelters antique dealers along its tiled corridors. Walk through and the tenement past is everywhere. Old porteno crowding, preserved.

hook: The narrowest house in Buenos Aires, just two and a half metres wide, wedged into a San Telmo passage.
facts: Located at Pasaje San Lorenzo 380 in San Telmo, Buenos Aires.
Its facade is about 2.5 metres wide while the house extends roughly 13 metres deep, making it the narrowest house in the city.
It was part of a larger home built in the 1820s, with a simple facade, a green door and an iron-barred balcony.
A 19th-century surveying error converting varas to metres effectively froze its dimensions and helped preserve it.
It is private property and cannot be entered, but the exterior is a noted local landmark.
legend: Popular tradition holds the narrow house was given to a freed slave by a former master; historical research treats this 'slave house' story as a myth rather than documented fact.
w: Barely two and a half metres wide, the slimmest house in Buenos Aires hides on a San Telmo passage, a sliver squeezed between its neighbours. Legend ties it to a freed slave handed the leftover strip of land, though historians doubt the tale. It belongs to the Zanjon's old plots. Blink on Pasaje San Lorenzo and you'll miss it.

hook: A German neo-Gothic memorial church facing Plaza Colombia, raised for a murdered heiress and tied to a school with hidden tunnels.
facts: Located at Isabel la Católica 520 (between Brandsen and Pinzón), facing Plaza Colombia in Barracas; the cultural-complex access is at Pinzón 1480.
It was designed by architect Ernesto Bunge in a German neo-Gothic style and built between 1872 and 1876, with sources noting a single-nave plan.
The church was raised in memory of Felicitas Guerrero de Álzaga (1846-1872), a wealthy young widow murdered on her estate by a rejected suitor, Enrique Ocampo.
Her family later donated the property to priests, who established the Colegio Santa Felicitas school and charitable works on the site.
The Complejo Histórico Santa Felicitas, including the church, an oratory, an 1893 hidden temple and tunnels, is visitable through guided tours.
legend: Local legend says the ghost of Felicitas appears each 30 January (the date of her death), weeping and dressed in white.
Folklore claims no weddings or baptisms are held in the church, calling it 'the church of misfortune'.
w: Behind a Barracas school gate stands a neo-Gothic chapel sealed since 1893, its altars and pews untouched, dust thick on the prayer books. Local legend tells of a priest who hanged himself here and a curse on weddings ever after. The hush feels lifted from a Gothic novel. Visit on a guided tour and the century-old silence does the rest.

hook: The last 'beach' of the Riachuelo: a forgotten strip of sand and rocks ringed by smokestacks of the Dock Sud petrochemical complex.
facts: Located at the mouth of the Riachuelo in Dock Sud, Avellaneda, inside the Polo Petroquimico de Dock Sud, beyond Villa Inflamable.
Roughly 5 km from Plaza de Mayo; reached by land via the Nicolas Avellaneda bridge into the Dock Sud port area.
Was a popular recreational bathing spot for residents of La Boca, Dock Sud and Isla Maciel until it was closed by the military dictatorship in 1976.
Now sits in a restricted zone supervised by security forces, surrounded by petrochemical plants whose smokestacks run continuously.
Coordinate corroborated by the Sandee map pin (-34.636206, -58.332563) and Google plus code 9M78+GX Dock Sud.
legend: Popularly called 'the last beach of the Rio de la Plata' / 'the lost beach of the Riachuelo'; an enthusiast collective ('Expediciones a Puerto Piojo') runs semi-clandestine pilgrimages to it.
w: Once the working-class 'beach' of the south, where families once swam by the Riachuelo before the refineries closed in, Puerto Piojo now lies a fenced-off ghost shore among the oil tanks of Dock Sud. Adventurers still slip past the wire to its ruined jetty. The last beach of the Plata, poisoned and abandoned. Go with a guide.

hook: An unmarked bookshop-cafe in a 1930s brick house with no shop window or sign: you ring the bell to enter a world of jazz, vinyl and French-press coffee.
facts: At Charlone 201, Chacarita, about six blocks from Chacarita Cemetery; the high-walled 1930s house has no street-facing window or signage, so visitors ring the doorbell.
Founded by Marcela Giscafre, who named it after the moths ('falenas') that recur in Virginia Woolf's writing.
Books are organised by country and include sections on art, photography, cinema, theatre, travel and international gastronomy.
Inside: leather armchairs, a wood-burning fireplace, a record player with large wooden speakers playing jazz, plus a mezzanine, basement and interior patio.
Open Monday to Friday afternoons (roughly 15:00-19:30), free entry; coffee and wine are served while you read.
w: No sign, no window display, just a bell on what looks like an abandoned Chacarita house. Ring it and you're let into Falena — 'moth' in Spanish — a candle-warm bookshop and cafe of crammed shelves, soft music and quiet readers. Part secret club, part literary refuge. Buenos Aires hides its best rooms behind plain doors.

hook: A hidden cocktail bar with no sign behind a narrow black door in a long blank wall: you slip through one person at a time into a 1930s house of crystal lamps and armchairs.
facts: At Arcos 2950, between Ibera and Quesada, on the Nunez/Belgrano border; the only street clue is a narrow black door in a long wall (sometimes a chalk number).
Set inside a 1930 house listed as Historical Heritage, with an open sector, a main bar room and lounge 'livings' in French and English style.
Serves signature cocktails (e.g. a Dark Fashion whiskey drink and citrus-forward gin cocktails) alongside a kitchen menu.
Programmes live music on Thursday nights.
Listed as an active venue on the City of Buenos Aires official 'secret bars' tourism site and on current delivery platforms (Rappi).
legend: Named for the 'luz mala' (evil light) of Argentine rural folklore: a ghostly glow said to hover over the pampas, often explained as marsh gas from buried bones.
w: Find the unmarked door in a long blank wall on a quiet Nunez street, and a dim, intimate cocktail bar opens up behind it. Luz Mala takes its name from the eerie 'bad light' said to flicker over the pampas at night, the glow of restless souls. Low light, good drinks, made for secrets and first dates. The trick is finding the door at all.

hook: A candlelit tasting-menu wine cellar hidden beneath a working laundromat: walk past the washing machines and fabric-softener smell, descend the stairs, and the city above disappears.
facts: At Maipu 872 in the Microcentro/downtown; the entrance is camouflaged as a laundry (industrial washers, laundry baskets) with the dining room hidden in the basement below.
Run by entrepreneurs Nery Aimale and Sebastian Antich, who returned to Buenos Aires from Barcelona in 2019.

Kitchen led by chef Tomas de Felice; the prix-fixe step menu changes by day, including a six-course/six-wine 'Experiencia Argentina, la ruta del vino' and a nine-course weekend 'Experiencia Bocabajo-Bocarriba'.
Holds a cellar of over 60 labels emphasising small Argentine producers across the country's wine regions, served in Riedel glassware.
Reservation-only; listed with current TripAdvisor reviews and an active Instagram (@bocabajo.bocarriba).
w: Push past the dryers of an ordinary downtown laundromat and you step into a clandestine dining room, all candlelight and tasting menus with Argentine wine. Bocabajo plays the speakeasy game in earnest — no sign, just the laundry out front. Book ahead and trust the disguise. Dinner theatre that starts with a load of washing.

hook: A Prohibition-style speakeasy hidden behind a classic pizzeria, built to look like an actual cobblestoned street running indoors under string lights.
facts: At Niceto Vega 4942, Palermo; entry is through La Guitarrita, a classic pizzeria on Avenida Niceto Vega, with no marquee or large windows out front.
The interior recreates a real street: cobblestone floor, high exposed-brick walls intervened with street art, kermesse lights and a long bar.
Signature cocktails are designed by acclaimed bartender Mona Gallosi and named after world cities (Tokyo, Corso Vittorio, Wellington).
Food comes from the adjoining La Guitarrita pizzeria (pizzas, mozzarella sticks, empanadas).
Open Mon-Fri 18:00-24:00 and Sat-Sun 16:00-24:00 per its official site; the bar displays the motto 'Chau, Ley Seca' nodding to US Prohibition.
A second location operates at Gurruchaga 732.
w: Walk through the back of a Palermo pizzeria and the 1920s open up: a Prohibition-era speakeasy with a cobblestoned 'street' floor, brass and dark wood, and cocktails named for local legends — one of them for Messi. The gimmick could be naff; instead it charms. Order the drink, admire the staging. A hidden street inside a bar.

hook: The world's first theatre where every performance happens in total, absolute darkness, stripping away sight so hearing, smell and touch take over, with an optional dine-in-the-dark menu.
facts: At Zelaya 3006, Abasto; the Centro Argentino de Teatro Ciego was created in 2008 as the first theatre worldwide staging all works in complete darkness.
Programme spans rock opera and music-heavy pieces plus spoken-word plays for adults and children, each over an hour long.
Most performers are blind or visually impaired, though the company casts both blind and sighted actors.
Offers 'A Ciegas Gourmet', a multi-course dinner eaten in total darkness.
Reachable by Subte via Corrientes, Cordoba or Carlos Gardel stations; tickets are sold through current platforms (e.g. Ticket Hoy), confirming active programming.
w: Theatre staged in absolute darkness, performed largely by blind and visually impaired artists, where sound, smell and touch carry the whole show. Teatro Ciego also serves dinners eaten blind, the flavours sharpened by the black. You surrender your eyes at the door. Unsettling, then exhilarating. Abasto's leap into the dark.

hook: A small specialty-coffee hideaway in Palermo with a leafy garden patio, beloved for its flat whites and iced lattes.
facts: At Gascon 1434, Palermo, Buenos Aires.
A cosy specialty-coffee cafe with both outdoor garden seating and indoor space, bright and welcoming decor.
Reviewers single out the flat white with oat milk and the cold-brew/iced lattes; regulars visit several times a week.
Maintains an active Facebook page and current positive reviews on travel/restaurant aggregators, indicating it is operating.
w: A specialty coffee spot with a leafy back garden on a quiet Palermo street, Ruta Jardin doubles as a little community hub — music sets, talks and workshops among the plants. Good beans, slow afternoons, the feel of a friend's overgrown patio. Far from the polished cafe circuit, and warmer for it. Order a flat white and stay.

hook: Mario Palanti's enigmatic 1920s 'round house' - a Dante-inspired tower nicknamed 'the little Barolo' - reborn as an exclusive restaurant in late 2025.
facts: At Ortiz de Ocampo 2901, Barrio Parque (Palermo Chico); built in 1922 by Mario Palanti, architect of the Palacio Barolo.
Originally the Petit Hotel Eduardo Resta, commissioned by the Resta family, owners of the Chrysler dealership across the street; figures of Dante and Beatrice are carved into the front gate.
Fuses styles under Art Deco arches and an ornate tower; in the 1970s it served as the Embassy of Iran under the Shah, when a reinforced basement bunker and escape passage were installed.
Reopened as the restaurant Casa Palanti in November 2025, with original floors, stained glass, mouldings and lamps preserved because the building is protected from structural change.
Operating hours listed as roughly 12:00-15:30 and 19:30-00:00 daily, with reservations via Meitre.
legend: Long known locally as 'the little Barolo' and rumoured to be themed on Dante's Divine Comedy, echoing Palanti's esoteric numerological design of the Palacio Barolo.
w: Mario Palanti, who dreamed up the Palacio Barolo, also built this round house in Barrio Parque — a tower with a bunker and escape passage, later the Shah-era Iranian embassy. Shut for decades, it reopened as a restaurant in 2025. Dine in a riddle of curved rooms and secret doors. Architecture at its most cloak-and-dagger.

hook: The famous corner cafe at San Juan & Boedo, named for the tango lyricist who wrote 'Sur' here.
facts: Stands at the traditional corner of Av. San Juan and Av. Boedo, with its entrance at Av. San Juan 3601 / Av. Boedo 999.
Declared a Cafe Notable in 2004 by the city's Comision de Cafes, Bares, Billares y Confiterias Notables (created under Ley 35 of 1998).
The corner is also within an Area de Proteccion Historica (APH) under Ley 67 of 1998.
The bar carried four names over its life: 'El Aeroplano', then 'Nippon', then 'Canadian', taking the name 'Esquina Homero Manzi' in 1981.
It closed on 6 March 1999 and reopened, renovated, in 2001.
As of May 2026 it faced a closure threat over a 150-million-peso labour court judgment, but remained open.
legend: Lyricist Homero Manzi is said to have written the tango 'Sur' at this corner in 1948 (later set to music by Anibal Troilo).
w: A grand corner cafe at the heart of Boedo, all mirrors, tango and red leather, named for the lyricist Homero Manzi, whose 'Sur' immortalised these streets. Whether he wrote here is more legend than fact, but Boedo claims him. Live tango most nights, medialunas by day. The golden age, kept warm on a busy corner.

hook: An 1904 corner cafe in Boedo with marble counter and checkerboard floors, said to be the birthplace of the sandwich de pavita.
facts: Housed in a building at Av. Boedo 857 raised in 1904 by Genoese builder-architect Lorenzo Berisso.
Before becoming a cafe the premises served as a candy shop, restaurant, pasta factory and confectionery.
Recognised as a Bar Notable of Buenos Aires and as 'Living Testimony of Citizen Memory' by the City Museum.
Keeps its original facade, high wood-beamed ceilings, exposed brick, marble counter and checkered floors.
Regulars over the years included legislator Alfredo Palacios, poet Raul Gonzalez Tunon, boxer Ringo Bonavena and footballer Jose Sanfilippo.
legend: Local lore credits the cafe with inventing the 'sandwich de pavita' (turkey sandwich), and claims Peron once had his car detour to stop here.
w: Open on Avenida Boedo since 1904, Margot has poured coffee for generations of bohemians, poets and pensioners under its old fans. Its name even gave the city a sandwich, of pastrami, cheese and peppers. Little has changed and that's precisely the point. A Boedo institution that refuses to modernise.

hook: A Barracas corner bar open continuously since 1942, its walls a museum of neighbourhood relics.
facts: Occupies the ground floor of a building at Av. Montes de Oca 1700/1702, on the corner of California, built in 1911 by architects Emilio Hugue and Vicente Colmegna.
The bar itself opened in 1942 and has operated without interruption ever since.
Recognised as a Bar Notable of Buenos Aires.
Bought in 1959 by Aureliano Moreno and Maria Licinia Tomas, whose family still runs it.
Has appeared in films including Eliseo Subiela's 'El lado oscuro del corazon' and the recent 'Puan'.
Interior keeps wooden swing doors, checkered floor, mirrors, antique cash registers and a wall of black-and-white photos.
w: A classic corner bodegon in industrial Barracas, trading from a handsome 1911 building since 1942, its zinc bar worn smooth by working-men's elbows. The neighbourhood rose and fell around it, and El Progreso kept pouring through it all. Vermouth, soda siphon, a fried milanesa. Barracas, undiluted.

hook: A Barracas almacen-turned-bar from 1893, a surviving temple of the old arrabal and tango's Guardia Vieja.
facts: Stands at Av. Iriarte 2290, on the corner of Goncalves Dias, in Barracas.
Opened on 11 October 1893 as a pulperia and general store, later becoming a cafe-bar with billiards.
Recognised as a Bar Notable of Buenos Aires, with over 130 years of history.
Keeps an enamelled sign honouring its founders Hidalgo, Gonzalez and Santamarina, three Spanish friends.
Closed during the pandemic and was bought and restored by Barracas locals Sergio Mosquera and Claudio Sodini, reopening in 2021.
legend: Said to have been a haunt of tango's Guardia Vieja, including Eduardo Arolas, Angel Villoldo, Agustin Bardi and Anselmo Aieta, all Barracas neighbours.
w: Founded as a pulperia in 1893, Los Laureles claims to be the oldest tavern still standing in Barracas — a low corner of dark wood and tango ghosts by the Riachuelo. Carlos Gardel is said to have sung in the back room. It still serves milanesas and nostalgia in equal measure. Porteno ritual, a century deep.

hook: An Almagro corner cafe since 1923, its walls covered with hundreds of football club pennants.
facts: Located at Guardia Vieja 3601, on the corner in Almagro.
Founded in 1923 by an Asturian immigrant, originally as 'El Asturiano Provisiones y Fiambreria', doubling as a neighbourhood store.
Declared a Cafe Notable de Buenos Aires in 2004.
Famous for its collection of more than 500 football-club pennants from around the world, begun by owner Don Mario as a tribute to River Plate.
w: A 1923 corner cafe in Almagro whose walls vanish behind hundreds of football pennants — banderines — sent from clubs around the world. Regulars have nursed coffees beneath the bunting for generations. Declared a bar notable, it keeps the communal spirit of old Buenos Aires alive. Order a cortado and read the flags.

hook: A 1939 bodegon at the 'eight corners' where three avenues cross, serving German fare with a tango pedigree.
facts: Sits at Av. Forest 1186, at the confluence of avenidas Forest, Elcano and Alvarez Thomas, which form eight corners on the Chacarita/Villa Ortuzar/Colegiales border.
Founded on 31 October 1939, originally as a confiteria and cafe, in an Art Deco building.
Declared a Bar Notable of Buenos Aires by the city's Ministry of Culture.
Gained a German imprint in the 1960s under a partner named Rojo; it has operated continuously and is famous for picadas and German sausages.
Open Monday-Thursday 8-23h and Friday-Saturday 8-24h as of 2025.
legend: Reputed regulars include tango figures Anibal Troilo, Homero Manzi, Osvaldo Pugliese and the Esposito brothers; Beba Pugliese composed the tango 'Mis 8 Esquinas' about it.
w: Where three avenues collide into an eight-cornered junction, this 1939 bar has anchored the crossroads ever since. Ocho Esquinas keeps the plain, generous spirit of an old porteno almacen — cheap wine, long talk, no airs. A bar notable on one of the city's odder intersections. Pull up a stool and stay a while.

hook: A 1927 Villa Devoto cafe famous for its abundant picadas, a haunt of Maradona that reopened after closing.
facts: Stands at Sanabria 3302, on the corner of Jose Pedro Varela in Villa Devoto.
Founded in 1927 by the couple Metodio Garcia and Carolina Urbina; the site had previously held a pharmacy.
Declared a Bar Notable and site of cultural interest in 1999.
Known for its homemade picada (shared platter).
Closed in July 2022 and reopened in 2024 under new operators, who added a rooftop terrace with a mural of Maradona.
legend: Said to have drawn famous visitors including Diego Maradona, Victor Hugo Morales, Graciela Borges and Francis Ford Coppola.
w: A wonderfully preserved 1927 cafe on a quiet Villa Devoto corner, all dark wood and football and tango memorabilia on the walls. Far from the centre in a leafy residential pocket, it draws devotees who rank it among the city's finest. Closed for a spell, it reopened in 2024. Worth the trek for the cortado and the calm.

hook: A Caballito cafe facing Parque Rivadavia where collectors of stamps, coins and medals gather, once called El Condor.
facts: Located on Av. Rivadavia at the corner of Florencio Balcarce, facing Parque Rivadavia in Caballito.
A cafe has stood on the site since around 1915-1920; it was earlier called 'El Pelicano' and then 'El Condor' before being renamed 'El Coleccionista'.
Recognised as a Bar Notable of Buenos Aires.
Its name honours its clientele of collectors of stamps, coins, banknotes and medals who gather, especially on Sunday mornings.
In the mid-1950s the Argentine Thematic Philatelic Association (AFITA) was founded here by twelve members.
legend: As 'El Condor' it is said to have been frequented by writer Conrado Nale Roxlo and occasionally visited by Roberto Arlt and painter Antonio Berni.
w: A 1920s corner bar in unflashy Caballito, crammed with the bric-a-brac its name promises — old radios, signs and photographs on every shelf and wall. Off any tourist radar, it pours for a loyal local crowd amid the clutter. The kind of place that wears its decades on its sleeve. Caballito soul, by the cupful.

hook: Buenos Aires' oldest bookshop, trading books on the same Monserrat corner since 1785, opposite the Manzana de las Luces.
facts: Located at Adolfo Alsina 500, on the corner of Bolívar, in Monserrat, opposite the Manzana de las Luces and the Iglesia de San Ignacio.
A bookselling business has operated on this corner since 1785, when the colonial-era 'Librería del Colegio' opened, making it the city's oldest bookshop.
It was relaunched under the name Librería de Ávila in 1994 by Miguel Ávila, who restored the historic premises.
The basement holds a small museum-style space and the shop specialises in old, rare and antiquarian books on Argentine history.
The city government lists it as a site of cultural interest and the first bookstore in Buenos Aires.
legend: Lore holds that 19th-century presidents Mitre, Sarmiento and Avellaneda, and later writers including Borges and Bioy Casares, were customers of the shop on this corner.
w: On the corner facing the Manzana de las Luces, the city's oldest bookshop has traded since 1785, when it served the Jesuit colleges across the street. Its crammed shelves hold rare volumes on Argentina and the Americas, old maps and out-of-print treasures. Browse where the Enlightenment shopped.

hook: Victorian-style bookshop, cafe and publisher in Palermo Hollywood, famed for its hushed back reading room of armchairs.
facts: Located at Honduras 5582 in Palermo Hollywood, near the corner of Fitz Roy.
Founded by Pablo Braun, it opened at the end of 2005 in a house he had bought that was originally slated for demolition.
It is conceived as a bookshop with a bar and patio, with a back reading room of armchairs for browsing purchases.
It stocks more than 40,000 fiction and essay titles.
In August 2008 it launched its own imprint, Eterna Cadencia Editora, making it a bookshop and publisher.
It runs the FILBA international literature festival, held across Buenos Aires, Montevideo and Santiago de Chile.
w: A handsome Palermo bookshop got up like a Victorian library — tall dark shelves, a cafe and a hushed back reading room over a garden. Eterna Cadencia also runs its own respected publishing house and a literary festival. Less a shop than a salon for the city's writers. Find the back room and lose an afternoon.

hook: Cavernous 1912 cafe-billiards hall in Villa Crespo, long an all-night refuge for pool, ping-pong and chess players.
facts: Located at Avenida Corrientes 5436 in Villa Crespo, occupying an 800 m2 premises.
Founded in 1912, it offers pool, billiards, foosball (metegol), ping-pong, chess and board games, with about ten pool tables and several ping-pong tables.
It was long known for being open 24 hours; since 2016 it closes around 4am rather than staying open round the clock.
The Buenos Aires Legislature declared it a site of Cultural Interest in 2012 and a Notable Bar (Bar Notable) of the city in 2014.
Scenes from the films Roma and La señal were shot inside the cafe.
legend: It is said that tango pianist Osvaldo Pugliese and poets Juan Gelman and Celedonio Flores were among its regulars.
w: A cavernous Villa Crespo bar that has stayed open late since 1912, its back rooms ringing with pool balls and ping-pong past midnight. It wears its scuffed, smoky charm without irony, a haunt of night owls. A protected bar notable, and a film set when the cameras want the real porteno night. Rack up and stay late.

hook: Riverside memorial park whose stone-stele Monument to the Victims of State Terrorism carves the names of the disappeared.
facts: It stretches over roughly 14 hectares along the Río de la Plata on Av. Costanera Norte Rafael Obligado, beside the Ciudad Universitaria in Núñez.
Its construction was approved by the Buenos Aires government on 21 July 1998, with commemorative sculptures installed from October 2006.
The Monument to the Victims of State Terrorism, designed after a 1998 competition won by architect Alberto Varas, consists of four concrete stelae set into the ground.
The stelae carry around 9,000 engraved names of people detained-disappeared or killed by the state in 1969-1983, arranged by year and age, with empty plaques left for unnamed victims.
The park is open to the public free of charge and includes outdoor sculptures by various artists along the river.
w: A long wound cut into the riverbank: walls of grey stone etched with some 9,000 names of the disappeared, sloping toward the Plata. Sculptures stand among the lawns; the water that hid so many bodies laps beyond. Quiet, severe and unbearably moving. Buenos Aires forcing itself to remember, in stone by the river.

hook: A 350-hectare urban wetland on reclaimed riverbed beside Puerto Madero, the city's largest and wildest green space.
facts: The reserve covers about 350 hectares on the Río de la Plata waterfront, the biggest and most biodiverse green space in the city of Buenos Aires.
Its main southern entrance is near the corner of Av. Tristán Achával Rodríguez and Av. Brasil, by the Lola Mora fountain; a northern entrance lies near Viamonte.
The land formed on rubble and silt deposited from the 1970s as part of an abandoned land-reclamation scheme, which was then colonised by wetland vegetation.
It has three lagoons and winding paths leading to the river, with meadows of pampas grass and groves of alder.
It hosts over 500 species of native plants plus abundant birds, mammals, reptiles and amphibians, and is recognised as a wetland of international importance (Ramsar site).
w: On riverbed reclaimed by failed land-fill, nature took over: 350 hectares of reedbeds and lagoons where coypu swim and 300 bird species nest, a wild green slab against the skyline. Porteno families walk and birdwatch its paths at weekends. The city's accidental wetland, right beside Puerto Madero. Bring binoculars.

hook: Sunday gaucho and folk fair in Mataderos, where folklore dance, criollo crafts and horseback ring-races fill the old stockyard streets.
facts: Held at the intersection of Av. Lisandro de la Torre and Av. de los Corrales, in front of the Museo Criollo de los Corrales in Mataderos.
It takes place on Sundays and national holidays, roughly from mid-March to mid-December, about 11:00 to 19:00.
The neighbourhood (its name means 'slaughterhouses') grew around the city's livestock market and abattoirs, where cattle from the provinces were brought to be slaughtered.
The fair stages folklore concerts, traditional dances, and gaucho skills including the carrera de sortijas (ring race) on horseback.
It has hundreds of stalls selling regional crafts and food, and the Buenos Aires Legislature has declared it Cultural Heritage of the City.
w: Out by the old stockyards in far-western Mataderos, the city's gaucho heart beats every Sunday: folk dancing, riders racing for the sortija ring, asado smoke, stalls of crafts and food. Started in 1986 to keep rural traditions alive, it draws portenos and provincials alike. The pampas, brought to the city's edge.

hook: Argentina's largest cemetery, a 95-hectare city of the dead whose most-visited tomb belongs to tango idol Carlos Gardel.
facts: Officially established on 9 December 1886, it covers about 95 hectares (230 acres), the largest cemetery in Argentina.
Its main entrance is on Av. Guzmán in the Chacarita neighbourhood.
It originated after the 1871 yellow-fever epidemic overwhelmed the city's existing cemeteries; it was first named Cementerio del Oeste before being renamed.
Tango singer Carlos Gardel's elaborate mausoleum is one of its most visited tombs, where fans place lit cigarettes in the bronze statue's hand.
Other notable burials include tango figures Aníbal Troilo, rock musician Gustavo Cerati, poet Alfonsina Storni and Nobel laureate Bernardo Houssay.
legend: Fans say Gardel's statue is given a fresh lit cigarette by visitors, especially around the anniversaries of his birth and death.
w: Bigger by far than tourist-famous Recoleta, Chacarita sprawls as the city's great democratic necropolis — 95 hectares of tree-lined avenues opened in 1886 after a yellow-fever epidemic. Carlos Gardel lies here; fans still wedge a lit cigarette into his bronze hand. Tango singers, presidents and ordinary portenos, side by side.
